exercises.title: fixedRate vs fixedDelay — execution timing
exercises.scenario: fixedRate triggers N ms from start; fixedDelay triggers N ms after completion.
exercises.instructions:
A job takes 8 seconds to run. At t=0 it starts.

With @Scheduled(fixedRate=10_000):
- When does the 2nd run START?

With @Scheduled(fixedDelay=10_000):
- When does the 2nd run START?

Which would cause overlapping runs if the job took 15 seconds?
exercises.expected_solution:
fixedRate: 2nd run starts at t=10s (10s after 1st started)
fixedDelay: 2nd run starts at t=18s (8s job + 10s delay after completion)
fixedRate would cause overlap if job takes 15s (2nd starts at t=10 while 1st ends at t=15)

test_cases.expected:
fixedRate: 2nd run starts at t=10s (10s after 1st started)
fixedDelay: 2nd run starts at t=18s (8s job + 10s delay after completion)
fixedRate would cause overlap if job takes 15s (2nd starts at t=10 while 1st ends at t=15)

hints: fixedRate: measured from START of previous execution
fixedDelay: measured from COMPLETION of previous execution

exercises.title: Enable async and add @Async method
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to enable async and mark the notification method.

blanks.hint: Marks method to run in background thread pool

hints: Without @EnableAsync, @Async is silently ignored
@Async on the method: Spring intercepts the call and runs it asynchronously

exercises.title: Decode cron expressions
exercises.scenario: Cron: second minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week
exercises.instructions:
Write the plain English meaning of each cron expression:

1. `0 0 * * * *`
2. `0 0 9 * * MON-FRI`
3. `0 0/30 * * * *`
4. `0 0 0 1 * *`
5. `0 0 18 * * FRI`
hints: Format: second minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week
0/30 = start at 0, repeat every 30
MON-FRI = range from Monday to Friday

exercises.title: @Async with CompletableFuture return type
exercises.scenario: CompletableFuture allows callers to await the async result.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `ReportService.generateReport(String userId)` that:
1. Is annotated @Async
2. Returns CompletableFuture<String>
3. Simulates a 2-second report generation with Thread.sleep
4. Returns the report string wrapped in CompletableFuture.completedFuture()

Show how the caller can use .get() to wait for the result.
hints: @Async + CompletableFuture<T>: runs async, result wrapped in CF
CompletableFuture.completedFuture(value): wraps a value in a completed CF
future.get(timeout, unit): waits up to timeout for the result

exercises.title: @Scheduled — three job types
exercises.scenario: Write the three common scheduling patterns.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `MarketJobs` @Component with:
1. Price refresh: every 30 seconds (fixedRate)
2. Queue processor: 60 seconds after completion (fixedDelay)
3. Market open: 9:15 AM weekdays (cron)
4. Market close: 3:30 PM weekdays (cron)

Each prints the current time and job name.
hints: fixedRate = 30_000: every 30 seconds from start
fixedDelay = 60_000: 60 seconds after completion
Cron for 9:15: '0 15 9 * * MON-FRI' (second=0, minute=15, hour=9)

exercises.title: Custom ThreadPoolTaskExecutor
exercises.scenario: Custom executor controls thread pool size and naming.
exercises.instructions:
Write an `AsyncConfig` @Configuration that creates two named executors:
1. 'notificationExecutor': coreSize=5, maxSize=20, queue=100, prefix='notif-'
2. 'reportExecutor': coreSize=2, maxSize=5, queue=10, prefix='report-'

Then show how to use them on @Async methods.
hints: @Bean('executorName'): named bean used in @Async('executorName')
ThreadPoolTaskExecutor: Spring's configurable thread pool
initialize() must be called after configuration

exercises.title: Fast HTTP response with @Async notification
exercises.scenario: Controller returns immediately while @Async sends notification in background.
exercises.instructions:
Build `OrderController.placeOrder()` that:
1. Saves the order via OrderService (synchronous, ~50ms)
2. Calls notificationService.sendEmail(order) — @Async, returns immediately
3. Returns 201 Created with the saved order
4. Verify: print thread name in both controller and notification service
hints: The controller and notification run on DIFFERENT threads
@Async returns void: fire-and-forget, no result needed
Thread.currentThread().getName() shows the difference

exercises.title: Self-invocation anti-pattern — and the fix
exercises.scenario: @Async doesn't work when called via 'this' in the same class.
exercises.instructions:
Show the self-invocation anti-pattern and the correct fix:

Anti-pattern: `OrderService.placeOrder()` calls `this.sendEmail()` which has @Async — doesn't work.

Fix: Extract sendEmail into a separate `EmailService` @Service and inject it.

Write both versions clearly labeled.
hints: this.method(): bypasses Spring AOP proxy → @Async ignored
Fix: extract @Async method to separate @Service, inject it
Spring proxy wraps the injected bean — calls through proxy work

exercises.title: Test @Async with CompletableFuture
exercises.scenario: Test async methods by waiting for the CompletableFuture result.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest test for `ReportService.generateReport(String userId)` (@Async returning CompletableFuture<String>):
1. Call the async method
2. Use future.get(10, SECONDS) to wait for the result
3. Assert the result contains the userId
hints: @SpringBootTest(NONE): loads Spring context without HTTP — @Async works
future.get(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS): wait up to 10s for async result
TimeoutException if async method takes > 10s

exercises.title: Thread names — sync vs async
exercises.scenario: Async methods run in a different thread than the caller.
exercises.expected_solution:
CONTROLLER on: http-nio-8080-exec-1
AFTER on: http-nio-8080-exec-1
EMAIL on: task-1 (or similar async thread name)

test_cases.expected:
CONTROLLER on: http-nio-8080-exec-1
AFTER on: http-nio-8080-exec-1
EMAIL on: task-1 (or similar async thread name)

hints: HTTP requests run on http-nio-*-exec-* threads
@Async methods run on Spring's task executor threads
AFTER prints on the same HTTP thread — sendEmail() returned immediately

exercises.title: Parallel async fan-out
exercises.scenario: Fan out multiple @Async calls and wait for all to complete.
exercises.instructions:
Write `DataAggregatorService.aggregatePortfolio(String userId)` that:
1. Calls three @Async services in parallel: fetchStockPrices, fetchMutualFunds, fetchBonds
2. Uses CompletableFuture.allOf() to wait for all three
3. Combines and returns a PortfolioSummary

Test: assert all three data types are present in the result.
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.concurrent.CompletableFuture;
// DataAggregatorService with 3 parallel @Async calls + allOf
hints: Three @Async CF calls launch simultaneously
CompletableFuture.allOf(f1, f2, f3).join(): wait for all three
After allOf: each future.get() is instant

exercises.title: Test @Scheduled by calling the method directly
exercises.scenario: Scheduled jobs are @Component methods — test them by direct call.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest test for `PriceRefreshJob.refreshPrices()` that:
1. @Autowired the job component
2. Calls refreshPrices() directly
3. Verifies the PriceService was invoked
4. Does NOT test the schedule timing — just the job logic
hints: Don't test the timing — just test what the job DOES when called
@Autowired the @Component directly in the test
Call job.method() directly — tests job logic, not schedule

exercises.title: @Async exception handling
exercises.scenario: Exceptions in void @Async methods are silently lost without a handler.
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate the exception problem and solution:
1. Show that exceptions in void @Async methods are lost without a handler
2. Implement an `AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler` that logs the exception
3. Register it in the `AsyncConfigurer`
4. Write a @SpringBootTest that triggers the exception and verifies it was logged
hints: AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler: catches exceptions from void @Async methods
AsyncConfigurer.getAsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler(): registers the handler
Thread.sleep(500) after calling async: gives it time to throw

exercises.title: Write cron expressions for Zerodha-style schedule
exercises.scenario: Real-world trading platform needs precise scheduling.
exercises.instructions:
Write @Scheduled methods for these Zerodha-like requirements:
1. Market data refresh: every 5 minutes during trading hours (9:15-15:30 on weekdays)
2. EOD settlement: 4:00 PM Mon-Fri
3. Monthly account statement: midnight on 1st of each month
4. Weekly portfolio summary: 9:00 AM every Monday
5. Heartbeat check: every 30 seconds (use fixedRate)
hints: 9-15: hours 9 through 15
0 0 0 1 * *: second=0, minute=0, hour=0, day=1, any month, any dow

exercises.title: Complete async order system — Module 14.2 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Production-ready order system with async notifications and scheduled jobs.
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete async order system:
- `NotificationService` with 3 @Async methods: sendConfirmation, notifyWarehouse, sendInvoice
- `OrderService.placeOrder()`: saves order, fires all 3 async — returns in <100ms
- `DailyReportJob` @Scheduled: 7:00 AM daily, generates report using OrderRepository
- Custom ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with core=5, max=15
- @SpringBootTest test: place order, verify DB persistence, verify notifications fired (within 2s)

All tests must pass.
hints: Three @Async calls from OrderService: all fire simultaneously
HTTP/service thread returns in <500ms despite 3 async tasks
Thread.sleep(1000) in test: gives async tasks time to complete before test ends
